name: Anubias nana 'Petite' ( 1 Pot)
description: Anubias barteri var. nana 'Petite' Care Guide Anubias nana 'Petite' Care Guide Anubias barteri var. nana 'Petite' is the miniature version of the already compact Anubias nana. It is one of the most desirable plants for nano-aquascaping and detailing larger layouts. Known for its tiny, deep green, teardrop-shaped leaves, it remains the "gold standard" for creating a sense of scale in miniature environments. For Aquatic Exotica , this is a high-demand "detailing" plant that is essential for professional-grade hardscape work. Lighting Light Intensity: Thrives in low to medium lighting . It is highly adaptable but does not require high-intensity light. Algae Risk: Because the leaves are tiny and grow extremely slowly, they are highly susceptible to Green Spot Algae (GSA) if placed directly under intense light. Placement: It is best used to detail the "cracks" and crevices of your hardscape. Placing it in slightly shaded areas or tucked between rocks helps keep the foliage clean. Water Parameters Hardiness: One of the hardiest plants in the hobby. It can withstand significant fluctuations in water chemistry. Temperature: Prefers 72°F to 82°F (22°C – 28°C) . pH Range: 6.0 – 7.5. It is comfortable in both soft and moderately hard water. Water Flow: Moderate flow is beneficial to prevent debris from settling on the small leaves, which can lead to localized rot or algae growth. CO2 and Fertilization CO2: Not required, making it perfect for low-tech "Walstad" or simple shrimp tanks. However, CO2 will produce a much denser, more compact growth habit. Fertilization: It is an epiphytic feeder. It primarily absorbs nutrients from the water column. Weekly dosing of a balanced liquid fertilizer is recommended. Nutrient Tip: If the tiny leaves start to turn yellow or show holes, it usually indicates a Potassium (K) or Iron (Fe) deficiency. Planting and Substrate The Critical Rule: Never bury the rhizome. If the green horizontal stem is buried under substrate, the plant will rot and die. Attachment: Its small size makes it perfect for "gluing." Use a tiny drop of cyanoacrylate super glue gel to attach it to Lava Rock, Driftwood, or Seiryu Stone . Detailing: Many scapers use 'Petite' to hide visible glue lines or to transition between large hardscape elements and the foreground substrate. Growth and Appearance Size: It is truly "petite," typically reaching only 1–2 inches (2.5–5 cm) in total height. The leaves are often no larger than a fingernail. Growth Rate: Extremely slow. It may only produce a few leaves per month. Durability: The leaves are thick and leathery, making them unappealing to most herbivorous fish and snails. Propagation Rhizome Division: Propagate by carefully cutting the thin rhizome with a sharp blade or specialized aquascaping scissors. Requirement: Each cutting should have at least 3 healthy leaves and a few root strands. Bushy Effect: Because it is so small, 'Petite' looks best when several small rhizomes are grouped together to form a dense "bush." General Tips Shrimp Safety: This plant is a favorite for shrimp breeders as it provides excellent surface area for biofilm to grow without taking up much swimming space. Maintenance: Use a soft toothbrush to very gently brush the leaves if you notice any algae buildup. Aquatic Exotica Design: Use 'Petite' in the foreground or to create "miniature forests" on driftwood branches. It pairs exceptionally well with Bucephalandra 'Mini Coin' for a high-end, textured look.

name: Peperomia caperata
description: Peperomia caperata Care Guide Peperomia caperata Care Guide Peperomia caperata, commonly known as Emerald Ripple Peperomia or Radiator Plant, is a charming compact houseplant native to Brazil. This popular species is prized for its distinctive heart-shaped leaves with deeply ridged, quilted texture that creates beautiful ripple patterns. The leaves are typically dark green with silvery veining, though many cultivars exist with variations in color including red, burgundy, and variegated forms. Lighting Peperomia caperata thrives in bright, indirect light conditions. Can tolerate medium light but may lose some vibrant coloration and compact growth. Avoid direct sunlight as it can scorch the delicate leaves and fade their patterns. Insufficient light causes elongated stems and loss of the characteristic compact form. Water Parameters Water when the top 1-2 inches of soil are dry, typically every 7-10 days. Peperomia caperata stores water in its thick leaves making it drought-tolerant. Water at soil level to avoid getting water on the textured leaves which can cause fungal issues. Ensure thorough drainage as waterlogged soil quickly leads to root rot. Reduce watering frequency in winter when growth naturally slows down. Overwatering is the most common cause of plant failure with this species. CO2 and Fertilization CO2 supplementation is not necessary for this houseplant. Feed monthly during spring and summer with diluted liquid fertilizer. Use balanced fertilizer (10-10-10) diluted to half strength to prevent burning. Reduce feeding to every 2-3 months during fall and winter dormancy. Planting and Substrate Requires extremely well-draining, lightweight potting mix to prevent root rot. Ideal soil blend: 50% quality potting soil, 30% perlite, 20% orchid bark. Choose pots with drainage holes; terracotta pots help prevent overwatering. Shallow pots are preferred as it has a small, fibrous root system. Growth and Appearance Peperomia caperata is a slow-growing, compact plant. Features heart-shaped leaves with deeply ridged, quilted texture. Leaves are typically 2-4 inches long with distinctive ripple patterns. Can reach a height and spread of 8-10 inches when mature. Produces small, cream-colored flower spikes on healthy plants. Many cultivars available with varying leaf colors from green to burgundy. Propagation Leaf cuttings are the most successful propagation method. Cut healthy leaves with petioles attached for best results. Allow cut surfaces to dry for 24 hours before planting. Root in well-draining mix under bright, indirect light. General Tips Peperomia caperata is relatively easy to care for and beginner-friendly. Sensitive to overwatering, so err on the side of underwatering. Remove yellowing or damaged leaves promptly to prevent disease spread. Clean leaves gently with a soft brush to remove dust from ridged surfaces. The plant is non-toxic to pets and children, making it household safe. Temperature range of 65-75°F (18-24°C) is ideal for growth.

name: Bucephalandra Pygmea (3 Rhizomes)
description: Bucephalandra "Pygmea" Care Guide Bucephalandra "Pygmea" Care Guide Bucephalandra "Pygmea" (also often spelled "Pygmaea" or "Pygmy") is a small, slow-growing species of Bucephalandra native to Borneo. It's valued for its diminutive size, often only 2-4 inches (5-10 cm) tall, and its wavy green leaves that grow in a rosette formation, making it a perfect addition to nano tanks and intricate aquascapes. Lighting Bucephalandra "Pygmea" thrives in low to moderate lighting conditions. While low light is sufficient for its survival, moderate lighting can enhance its coloration and growth. However, be cautious with high lighting levels as it is a slow grower and susceptible to algae growth on its leaves. Some varieties, like 'Wavy green', require moderate to high lighting, but it's important to balance light intensity and duration to avoid stressing the plants or encouraging algae growth. Water Parameters Bucephalandra "Pygmea" prefers stable water conditions. Ideal temperature range: 71-82.5°F (21.5-28°C) or 72-82°F (22-28°C). pH range: 5.5-7.5 or 6.0-7.5. Maintain clean, well-filtered water with proper flow. Bucephalandra species do better in stable tanks with matured biological systems. Some hardness (GH) is beneficial; preferably 5 GH and above. CO2 and Fertilization CO2 injection is not mandatory for survival but can significantly enhance growth and coloration. Bucephalandra primarily absorbs nutrients from the water column, so a liquid fertilizer can be beneficial. Regular dosing of a comprehensive liquid fertilizer will support healthy development. Adequate fertilization is important, especially with a balanced NPK + Fe (nitrogen, phosphorus, potassium + iron) fertilizer. Planting and Substrate Bucephalandra "Pygmea" is an epiphytic plant and should not have its rhizome buried in the substrate, as this can lead to rot. Attach it to hardscape, such as rocks or driftwood, using super glue (gel type recommended) or thread. If you choose to place it in the substrate, ensure only the roots are covered and the rhizome remains exposed. Growth and Appearance Bucephalandra "Pygmea" is a slow-growing plant. It typically reaches a size of 2-4 inches (5-10 cm) tall. Features wavy, green leaves that grow in a rosette formation. Can exhibit rich, dark green hues, and some varieties may have subtle red or brown undertones. Propagation Propagate by dividing the rhizome into sections. Each section should have healthy roots and at least one growing tip. Reattach these sections to hardscape as you would with a new plant. General Tips Bucephalandra is generally considered easy to care for. Maintain stable water parameters to prevent melting. Melting can occur when introduced to a new environment, especially in uncycled tanks, but the plant usually recovers and produces new growth from the rhizome if the rhizome remains healthy. Regularly remove any yellowing, decaying, or melting leaves. Good water flow is beneficial.

name: Bucephalandra Mini Velvet
description: Bucephalandra "Mini Velvet" Care Guide Bucephalandra "Mini Velvet" Care Guide Bucephalandra "Mini Velvet" is a captivating and highly sought-after variety of Bucephalandra, known for its deep red or maroon coloration and velvety leaf texture. Native to Borneo, these slow-growing plants add a vibrant and unique element to planted aquariums. Lighting Bucephalandra "Mini Velvet" can thrive in low to medium lighting conditions. Moderate lighting is recommended to bring out its best colors and encourage healthy growth. While it can tolerate higher lighting, be mindful of algae growth, as Bucephalandra are slow growers. Excessive light can cause leaves to burn and stress the plant, especially without adequate CO2 and nutrients. Water Parameters Bucephalandra "Mini Velvet" prefers stable water conditions. Ideal temperature range: 70-82°F (21-28°C) or {Link: 71-82.5°F (21.5-28°C) according to Shrimp and Snail Breeder https://aquariumbreeder.com/bucephalandra-care-guide-planting-growing-and-propagation/}. pH range: 6.0-7.5. Good water flow is beneficial, mimicking its natural habitat in streams with moderate to high flow. Maintain clean, well-filtered water with regular water changes to prevent the buildup of waste products. Some hardness (GH/KH) is helpful, with a dGH of 5 or above being recommended. CO2 and Fertilization CO2 injection is not essential for survival, but it will significantly enhance growth and coloration. Bucephalandra primarily absorbs nutrients from the water column, so a liquid fertilizer is beneficial. Regular fertilization with a comprehensive liquid fertilizer containing N, P, K, and micronutrients like iron and magnesium, will support healthy growth and vibrant colors. Planting and Substrate Bucephalandra "Mini Velvet" is an epiphytic plant and should not have its rhizome buried in the substrate, as this can lead to rot and melting. Attach it to hardscape elements like rocks or driftwood using super glue (gel type recommended) or thread. If planting in the substrate, ensure only the roots are covered and the rhizome remains exposed. Growth and Appearance Bucephalandra "Mini Velvet" is a slow-growing plant. It features deep red or maroon leaves with a velvety texture. The leaves are generally 4-6 cm long and 2-4 cm wide, often with wavy edges. Young leaves may be a lighter color, deepening to a rich red or maroon as they mature. It can reach a height of 10-15 cm (4-6 inches). Some varieties may also show iridescent qualities, reflecting blue or purple hues. Propagation Propagate by cutting the rhizome into sections, each with a few leaves and roots. Reattach these sections to hardscape or plant them (with the rhizome exposed) in the substrate. Bare rhizomes can also produce new shoots if healthy and provided with good conditions. General Tips Bucephalandra "Mini Velvet" is relatively hardy and considered easy to care for, even for beginners. However, it is sensitive to drastic changes in water parameters, so maintaining stability is key. Melting can occur when introduced to a new environment or with unstable parameters, but new growth will typically emerge from a healthy rhizome. Regularly remove any yellowing, decaying, or melting leaves. Good water flow is crucial for distributing nutrients and preventing detritus buildup. ```

name: Bucephalandra Boyan (3 Rhizomes)
description: Bucephalandra "Boyan" Care Guide Bucephalandra "Boyan" Care Guide Bucephalandra "Boyan" is a slow-growing variety of Bucephalandra that is relatively easy to care for, making it suitable for both beginner and experienced aquascapers. While low light is sufficient for survival, moderate lighting can promote faster growth and enhance coloration. If using higher light levels, be mindful of potential algae growth, as Bucephalandra grows slowly. Some aquascapers place Bucephalandra in shaded areas to help prevent algae issues. Water parameters Maintain stable water parameters for optimal health. Ideal temperature range: 71-79°F (21-26°C), although they can tolerate a wider range of 70-82°F (21-28°C). pH range: 6.0-8.0. Good water flow is beneficial, mimicking its natural habitat in fast-moving streams. Clean, well-filtered water with regular water changes is essential. Some water hardness (GH) is helpful, preferably 5 dGH or above. CO2 and Fertilization CO2 injection is not strictly required but can promote faster growth and more vibrant coloration. Bucephalandra primarily absorbs nutrients from the water column, so a liquid fertilizer can be beneficial. Regular fertilization with a comprehensive liquid fertilizer is recommended, particularly if using CO2 injection. Planting and Substrate "Boyan" is an epiphytic plant and should not have its rhizome buried in the substrate, as this can cause it to rot. Attach it to hardscape, such as rocks or driftwood, using super glue (gel type recommended) or thread. If placing it in the substrate, ensure the roots are covered but the rhizome remains exposed. Growth and Appearance Bucephalandra "Boyan" is a slow-growing plant. It typically grows up to 3-6 cm (1.5-2.5 inches) tall. Some varieties, like "Mini Narrow," are known for their compact size. The leaves can have a distinctive shape, with round or elongated forms and potentially wavy edges. Its final size, coloration, and growth rate depend on the aquarium conditions. Propagation Propagate by cutting the rhizome into sections using clean, sharp scissors. Ensure each section has healthy roots and a few leaves or a growing tip for best results. Reattach these sections to hardscape or plant them (with the rhizome exposed) in the substrate. General Tips Bucephalandra "Boyan" is considered easy to moderate in care level. It is sensitive to sudden changes in water parameters, so aim for stability. Melting can occur when the plant adjusts to a new environment, but it will usually recover and produce new growth from the rhizome if it remains healthy. Regularly remove any dead, yellowing, or decaying leaves to encourage new growth. Bucephalandra can be grown emersed (out of water) in humid environments like paludariums or terrariums. ```

name: Bucephalandra "Neo" (1 Rhizome)
description: Bucephalandra "Neo" Care Guide Bucephalandra "Neo" Care Guide Bucephalandra "Neo" is a distinguished and prestigious Premium Buce variety , prized for its exceptionally dense growth and brilliant coloration. A true collector's item at Aquatic Exotica , "Neo" is famous for its vibrant, almost neon-blue and deep purple iridescence that coats its dark foliage. It is a compact variety that brings a sophisticated, high-end feel to professional aquascapes, standing out as one of the most colorful specimens in the genus. Lighting Intensity: Thrives in medium to high lighting . High intensity is the key to activating the "Neo" metallic blue and violet pigments. Spectrum: Professional RGB LED lighting is essential to reflect the complex iridescent layers of the leaves. Photoperiod: A stable 7–9 hour light cycle is recommended. Ensure balanced nutrients to prevent algae from settling on its slow-growing leaves. Water Parameters Stability: Like all top-tier premium varieties, "Neo" requires very stable water chemistry. Avoid large, sudden changes to prevent rhizome shock. Temperature: Prefers 71°F to 78°F (22°C – 26°C) . Consistency is vital for maintaining the plant's health. pH Range: 6.0 – 7.4. It performs best in soft to medium-hard, slightly acidic water. Flow: Enjoys moderate to strong water circulation, which keeps the leaf surfaces clean and delivers vital nutrients. CO2 and Fertilization CO2: Highly recommended. CO2 injection is necessary to achieve the maximum color saturation and the dense, bushy growth that "Neo" is known for. Fertilization: It is a water-column feeder. Regular dosing of liquid macros and micros is essential. Iron (Fe) and Trace elements are critical for the blue/purple metallic sheen. Growth Rate: Very slow. This slow progression makes established, multi-headed clumps highly valuable. Planting and Substrate Epiphytic Growth: Do not bury the rhizome. The rhizome must remain exposed to the water column. If buried in soil or gravel, it will rot. Attachment: Secure to Lava Rock, Driftwood, or specialized Hardscape using super glue gel or dark cotton thread. Placement: Its intense color and compact size make it a perfect foreground accent or a "jewel" tucked into the midground. Appearance and Texture Leaf Shape: Small, rounded to slightly oval leaves with a thick, leathery texture. Coloration: Displays a dark base color that transforms into a brilliant neon-blue or purple "oil-slick" sheen under optimal conditions. Stomata: Features a dense coating of tiny silver dots (stomata), creating a "galaxy" effect across the colorful leaves. Propagation Rhizome Division: Propagate by carefully cutting the rhizome with a sharp, sterile blade. Requirement: Each cutting should have at least 3–5 healthy leaves to ensure a successful transition. Branching: Trimming the lead tip encourages the plant to sprout new lateral heads, eventually forming a thick, colorful cushion. General Tips Melt Management: If the plant experiences melt after introduction, keep the water stable and do not move the rhizome. New growth will eventually emerge. Maintenance Crew: Amano shrimp and Otocinclus are vital for keeping the delicate leaves free of biofilm and algae. Aquatic Exotica Design: Use "Neo" as a high-contrast focal point against lighter stones like Seiryu or light green mosses to make the blue hues "pop."

name: Bucephalandra Super Mini Coin
description: Bucephalandra "Super Mini Coin" Care Guide Bucephalandra "Super Mini Coin" Care Guide Bucephalandra "Super Mini Coin" is an elite Premium Buce variety and represents one of the smallest known cultivars in the hobby. Characterized by its tiny, perfectly rounded leaves that resemble miniature green coins, it is a masterpiece of natural geometry. This variety is a signature offering for high-end detailing, providing a unique scale and texture that allows for the creation of intricate, sprawling "cushions" over hardscape surfaces. Lighting Intensity: Thrives in medium to high lighting . While it can survive in lower light, high-intensity light is required to maintain its extremely compact growth habit and to encourage the metallic "shimmer" on its leaf surfaces. Spectrum: Professional-grade RGB LED lighting is recommended to highlight the silvery "galaxy" spots (stomata) that are visible even on its tiny foliage. Photoperiod: A stable cycle of 7–8 hours is ideal. Due to its exceptionally slow growth, it is susceptible to Green Spot Algae (GSA) if lighting is excessive without balanced CO2. Water Parameters Stability: Demands strictly consistent water chemistry. Like other top-tier premium varieties, it is highly sensitive to sudden shifts in GH, KH, or pH, which can trigger rhizome melt. Temperature: Prefers cooler tropical water between 22°C – 26°C . pH Range: 6.0 – 7.4. Performs optimally in soft to medium-hard, slightly acidic water. Water Flow: Enjoys moderate to strong flow . Good circulation keeps the tiny, dense clusters free of organic debris and ensures efficient nutrient delivery. CO2 and Fertilization CO2: Highly recommended. CO2 injection is the key to achieving a dense, lush habit and encouraging the miniature rhizome to branch effectively into a compact mound. Fertilization: Primarily a water-column feeder. Regular dosing of liquid macros and micros (NPK + Trace) is vital. Iron (Fe) is important for maintaining its deep, healthy green luster. Growth Rate: Extremely slow. This slow progression makes large, established colonies of "Super Mini Coin" a rare and highly valuable addition to any professional layout. Planting and Substrate The Epiphyte Rule: Never bury the rhizome. The horizontal stem must remain exposed to the water column; burying it in substrate will lead to rot and plant death. Attachment: Its tiny size makes it perfect for "gluing." Use a small amount of cyanoacrylate super glue gel or dark thread to secure it to Lava Rock, Driftwood, or Seiryu Stone . Placement: Due to its miniature scale, it is best placed in the foreground or used as a "detail plant" in the tightest cracks of your hardscape to simulate natural growth patterns. Appearance and Texture Leaf Shape: Tiny, circular to slightly oval leaves that remain compact and close to the rhizome. Coloration: Displays a rich emerald to forest green base. Under optimal conditions, it can exhibit a subtle metallic blue sheen. Texture: Features a dense coating of tiny silver "galaxy" spots (stomata) that sparkle under high-quality aquarium lights. Propagation Rhizome Division: Propagate by carefully cutting the rhizome into sections with a sharp, sterile blade. Requirement: Each cutting should have at least 5–6 healthy leaves. Because the plant is so small, handle it with extreme care to avoid crushing the delicate rhizome. Form: Trimming the lead growing point often stimulates dormant buds along the rhizome, encouraging the plant to form a thicker, more impressive "carpet" over the hardscape. General Tips Melt Management: If transition melt occurs, maintain stable conditions and do not move the rhizome. New, water-adapted growth will eventually emerge from a firm rhizome. Maintenance Crew: Amano shrimp and Otocinclus are essential for keeping the slow-growing, intricate leaves free of biofilm and algae. Design Tip: As shown in your setup, pairing this miniature green variety with vibrant Fittonia Pink creates a spectacular contrast in color, scale, and leaf structure.

name: Bucephalandra Mini Dark
description: Bucephalandra "Mini Dark" Care Guide Bucephalandra "Mini Dark" Care Guide Bucephalandra "Mini Dark" is a prestigious and highly sought-after Premium Buce variety prized for its miniature scale and intense, moody coloration. In the Aquatic Exotica collection, this variety is celebrated for its small, elliptical leaves that can develop a deep midnight-green to charcoal base, shimmering with a striking metallic blue or purple iridescence under professional-grade lighting. Its compact growth habit makes it the ultimate "detail plant" for high-end nano tanks and intricate professional aquascapes. Lighting Intensity: Thrives in medium to high lighting . While it can tolerate lower light, higher intensity is required to unlock the deep "Dark" metallic pigments and the dense silver "galaxy" stomata on the leaf surface. Spectrum: High-quality RGB LED lighting is highly recommended to highlight the complex iridescent layers and the velvety texture of the foliage. Photoperiod: Maintain a stable cycle of 7–8 hours. As a slow-growing premium variety, it is susceptible to Green Spot Algae (GSA) if lighting is excessive without balanced nutrients. Water Parameters Stability: Demands strictly consistent water chemistry. Like other top-tier Borneo species, "Mini Dark" is sensitive to sudden shifts in GH, KH, or pH, which can trigger rhizome melt. Temperature: Prefers cooler tropical water, ideally 71°F to 78°F (22°C – 26°C) . pH Range: 6.0 – 7.4. It performs best in soft to medium-hard, slightly acidic to neutral water. Water Flow: Enjoys moderate to strong flow . Good circulation keeps the dense, miniature clusters free of organic debris and biofilm. CO2 and Fertilization CO2: Highly recommended. CO2 injection is essential for achieving maximum color saturation and encouraging the tiny rhizome to branch into a dense, bushy mound. Fertilization: Primarily a water-column feeder. Regular dosing of liquid macros and micros (NPK + Trace) is vital. Iron (Fe) is particularly important for maintaining the deep, dark metallic tones. Growth Rate: Extremely slow. This slow progression makes established, multi-headed clumps highly valuable and a significant milestone for any collector. Planting and Substrate The Epiphyte Rule: Do not bury the rhizome. The horizontal stem must remain exposed to the water column; burying it in substrate will lead to rot and plant death. Attachment: Its tiny size makes it perfect for "gluing." Use a small amount of cyanoacrylate super glue gel or dark thread to secure it to Lava Rock, Driftwood, or Seiryu Stone . Placement: Due to its miniature scale, it is best placed in the foreground or used as a "jewel" tucked into the tightest cracks of your hardscape to provide a sense of age and intricate detail. Appearance and Texture Leaf Shape: Tiny, oval to elliptical leaves with a thick, leathery texture and slightly wavy margins. Coloration: Displays a dark forest green to charcoal base color. Under optimal conditions, it glows with a metallic blue or purple "oil-slick" sheen. Texture: Features a coating of tiny silver "galaxy" spots (stomata) that sparkle under high-quality aquarium lights. Propagation Rhizome Division: Propagate by carefully cutting the rhizome into sections with a sharp, sterile blade. Requirement: Each cutting should have at least 4–5 healthy leaves to support a successful transition. Form: Trimming the lead growing point often stimulates dormant buds along the rhizome, encouraging the plant to form a thicker, more impressive cushion. General Tips Melt Management: If transition melt occurs, maintain stable conditions and do not move the rhizome. New, water-adapted growth will eventually emerge from a firm rhizome. Maintenance Crew: Amano shrimp and Otocinclus are essential for keeping the slow-growing, intricate leaves polished and free of biofilm. Design Tip: As seen in your setup, "Mini Dark" creates a stunning contrast when paired with vibrant, larger-leaved plants like Fittonia Pink .

name: Aridarum Narrow
description: Aridarum Narrow Care Guide Aridarum Narrow Care Guide Aridarum Narrow is a rare and unique plant that thrives in fast-flowing currents of waterfalls and streams in its natural habitat, which is the island of Borneo. It can be grown submerged in an aquarium or emersed (above water) in a terrarium or paludarium. General Care Requirements Lighting: Low to medium lighting is preferred. Excessive light can encourage algae growth. Water Flow: Aridarum Narrow does well with good water flow, similar to its natural habitat in fast-moving streams. Planting: This is an epiphytic plant, meaning it grows attached to surfaces like driftwood or rocks, not planted in the substrate. Burying the rhizome can cause it to rot and die. You can attach it using thread, super glue, or by wedging it into crevices. Growth Rate: Aridarum Narrow is known for its slow growth rate. Propagation: Propagate by cutting the rhizome and attaching the sections to hardscape. CO2: CO2 injection is recommended for better growth, especially in aquarium settings. Specific Care for Aquariums Water Parameters: Stable water parameters are essential for healthy growth. Avoid drastic changes, which can lead to melting or rotting. Rhizome Placement: Never bury the rhizome in the substrate; it should be exposed and attached to hardscape. Specific Care for Terrariums/Paludariums Humidity: High humidity (80% or more) is crucial for this plant. Placement: It grows beautifully when attached to a rock or the back wall of a terrarium. Moisture: Keep the plant permanently moist, but it can tolerate short periods of drought. Fertilizer: Fertilize sparingly with a diluted orchid fertilizer during the growing season. Light: Aridarum Narrow prefers a semi-shaded location in emersed setups. Important Notes Aridarum Narrow is similar in care to Bucephalandra and Java Fern species. Ensure you remove the plant from its pot and any cotton surrounding the roots before placing it in your setup. Do not treat it like a traditional rooted aquatic plant; the rhizome needs to be above the substrate. By following these guidelines, you can successfully cultivate this unique and visually appealing plant in your aquarium or terrarium setup.
