name: Fittonia (Green, Single Plant)
description: Fittonia (Nerve Plant) Care Guide Fittonia (Nerve Plant) Care Guide Fittonia , commonly known as the Nerve Plant or Mosaic Plant , is a striking tropical perennial native to the rainforests of South America. It is famous for its deeply veined leaves that create a high-contrast "nerve" pattern. While they are popular houseplants, their intense love for humidity makes them the absolute superstars of the closed terrarium world. They are also known as the "Drama Queens" of plants because they will dramatically wilt the moment they get too dry, only to "resurrect" within hours of being watered. Lighting Fittonia prefers bright, indirect light . In their native habitat, they grow on the forest floor under a dense canopy. Direct Sun: Strictly avoid direct sunlight; the thin, delicate leaves will scorch or develop bleached spots almost instantly. Low Light: They can tolerate lower light, but the vibrant colors of the veins (pink, red, or white) may fade, and the plant will become "leggy" as it stretches for light. Watering and Humidity Humidity: Requires high humidity (60-90%) . If the air is too dry, leaf edges will turn crisp and brown. This is why they thrive so well in glass enclosures. Watering: Keep the soil consistently moist but not waterlogged. They do not like to dry out between waterings. The "Faint": If you see your Fittonia collapsed flat against the soil, it is thirsty. Give it a thorough drink, and it should perk back up quickly. Water Quality: Sensitive to chlorine. Using room-temperature distilled or rainwater helps prevent leaf tip burn. Temperature Ideal temperature range: 65-80°F (18-27°C). As a true tropical, it is very sensitive to cold. Keep it away from drafty windows or air conditioning vents. Temperatures below 55°F (13°C) can be fatal. Soil and Fertilization Substrate: Use a well-draining potting mix that retains moisture. A blend of peat moss or coco coir with perlite is ideal. Fertilizer: Feed every 4–6 weeks during the growing season (spring/summer) with a balanced liquid fertilizer diluted to half-strength. Growth and Appearance Growth Habit: Low-growing and spreading. It rarely exceeds 6 inches in height but can trail beautifully. Varieties: Argyroneura: Silver-white veins on dark green leaves. Pearcei: Bright pink or red veins on olive-green leaves. Frankie: Primarily pink leaves with green edges. Propagation Propagation is incredibly easy through stem cuttings. Snip a stem with at least two nodes and place it in a jar of water or directly into moist soil. In a high-humidity environment, roots will usually appear within 1–2 weeks. General Tips Pruning: To keep the plant bushy, pinch off the tips of the stems regularly. This encourages horizontal growth. Flowers: Fittonia can produce small flower spikes. Many growers snip these off so the plant directs its energy toward its foliage. Terrarium Companion: Fittonia pairs perfectly with Mood Moss and Ferns, as they all share identical humidity and light requirements.
source_categories: Indoor Plants | Terrarium Plants

name: Anubias nana 'Gold' (1 Pot)
description: Anubias barteri var. nana 'Gold' Care Guide Anubias nana 'Gold' Care Guide Anubias nana 'Gold' (also known as Anubias 'Golden') is a vibrant color variant of the classic Anubias nana. It is highly prized in the aquascaping community for its striking lime-green to golden-yellow foliage. Unlike the standard dark green Anubias, the 'Gold' variety provides a brilliant "pop" of color that can brighten up dark corners of an aquarium. It is just as hardy as the original species, making it an excellent choice for Aquatic Exotica customers who want a high-contrast look without the difficulty of demanding stem plants. Lighting Light Intensity: Prefers low to medium lighting . While its golden color can become more intense under medium light, it should be monitored closely. Algae Risk: Because it is a slow grower, high intensity light can quickly lead to Green Spot Algae (GSA) covering the leaves. If the leaves start turning green rather than gold, it is often a sign of too little light; if they turn white or transparent, the light is too intense. Placement: Ideally placed in the midground or foreground where it is not overshadowed but is protected from the most intense "hot spots" of your aquarium LED. Water Parameters Adaptability: Extremely hardy and tolerant of a wide variety of water conditions. Temperature: Prefers 72°F to 82°F (22°C – 28°C) . Like most Anubias, it can handle occasional temperature fluctuations well. pH Range: 6.0 – 7.8. It thrives in both soft and moderately hard water. Water Flow: Appreciates moderate water flow to keep the leaves free of detritus and waste buildup. CO2 and Fertilization CO2: Not required for survival, but CO2 injection will encourage faster growth and more vibrant golden hues in new leaves. Fertilization: It draws nutrients from the water column through its leaves and roots. Regular dosing of a comprehensive liquid fertilizer is recommended. Nutrient Tip: Maintaining proper phosphate levels can help keep the slow-growing leaves free from Green Spot Algae. Planting and Substrate The Golden Rule: Do not bury the rhizome. The thick horizontal stem must remain above the substrate. If buried in sand or gravel, the rhizome will rot and the plant will eventually die. Attachment: Best used as an epiphyte. Secure it to Lava Rocks, Driftwood, or Seiryu Stones using cyanoacrylate super glue gel or dark thread. Its roots will naturally grow and anchor themselves to the surface over time. Growth and Appearance Size: A compact variety, typically reaching 2–4 inches (5–10 cm) in height. Coloration: New leaves appear as a bright, neon yellow-gold, gradually maturing into a lime-green. This creates a beautiful gradient of color within a single clump. Growth Rate: Very slow. It is a patient plant that requires very little maintenance once established. Propagation Rhizome Division: Simply cut the rhizome into pieces with a sharp, clean blade. Requirement: Ensure each section has at least 3–4 healthy leaves and a few root filaments. Bushy Growth: Trimming the lead tip can help stimulate the rhizome to branch out, creating a fuller, more compact golden bush. General Tips Maintenance: Occasionally "dust" the leaves during water changes by gently rubbing them between your fingers to remove biofilm or early algae growth. Clean-up Crew: Amano shrimp, Nerite snails, and Otocinclus are perfect for keeping the bright golden leaves polished and clean. Design Tip: For Aquatic Exotica setups, pair 'Gold' Anubias next to dark-colored plants like Bucephalandra 'Godzilla' or Dark Catherine . The color contrast is spectacular and makes both plants stand out.
source_categories: Aquatic Plants | Rhizome plants | Terrarium Plants

name: Anubias nana ( 1 Pot)
description: Anubias barteri var. nana Care Guide Anubias nana Care Guide Anubias barteri var. nana , or simply Anubias nana , is perhaps the most iconic and essential plant in the freshwater aquarium hobby. Native to the shaded riverbanks of West Africa, it is legendary for its "bulletproof" nature. It features thick, dark green, heart-shaped leaves that are tough enough to withstand even the most herbivorous fish, such as Cichlids or Goldfish. For a business like Aquatic Exotica , it is a staple "bread and butter" plant that appeals to both beginners and high-end scapers. Lighting Light Intensity: Prefers low to medium lighting . It is one of the few plants that can thrive in very dim setups. Direct Sun/High Light: In high-tech tanks with intense light, Anubias nana is highly prone to Green Spot Algae (GSA) because its leaves are very slow-growing and long-lived. Placement: It is best placed in shaded areas under larger plants or hardscape to keep the leaves clean and algae-free. Water Parameters Hardiness: Extremely adaptable to a wide range of water conditions. Temperature: Prefers 72°F to 82°F (22°C – 28°C) . It can tolerate warmer water better than many Bucephalandra species. pH Range: 6.0 – 7.5. It is comfortable in both soft and moderately hard water. Flow: Appreciates moderate water movement to prevent organic debris from settling on its broad leaves, which can suffocate the plant or encourage algae. CO2 and Fertilization CO2: Not required. It is a true "low-tech" plant. However, CO2 injection will result in faster leaf production and more robust growth. Fertilization: As an epiphyte, it takes its nutrients from the water column. Regular dosing of a comprehensive liquid fertilizer is beneficial. Nutrient Warning: If the leaves turn yellow (chlorosis), it is often a sign of iron or potassium deficiency. Planting and Substrate The Golden Rule: Do not bury the rhizome. If the thick, horizontal stem (rhizome) is buried in sand or gravel, it will rot, and the plant will die. Attachment: Secure the plant to Driftwood, Bogwood, or Rocks using super glue gel, fishing line, or cotton thread. Over time, its strong roots will anchor itself firmly to the hardscape. Substrate Option: You can bury the fine roots in the substrate, but ensure the rhizome itself remains entirely above the surface. Growth and Appearance Size: A compact variety that reaches about 2–4 inches (5–10 cm) in height. Growth Rate: Very slow. It typically produces one new leaf every few weeks. Foliage: Thick, leathery, and dark green. The leaves are remarkably durable and can last for years in the right conditions. Propagation Rhizome Division: Easily propagated by cutting the rhizome with a sharp, clean pair of scissors or a blade. Requirement: Each division should have at least 3–4 healthy leaves and a few roots to ensure survival. Bushy Growth: To encourage the plant to branch out, you can make a small nick in the rhizome; this often stimulates a new growing point. General Tips Algae Prevention: To combat Green Spot Algae, maintain a healthy level of Phosphates in the water and keep the plant in lower-light areas of the tank. Clean-up Crew: Nerite snails and Otocinclus are excellent for keeping the broad, flat leaves of Anubias nana polished and free of biofilm. Aquatic Exotica Design: This plant is perfect for the midground or for covering the "joints" where driftwood meets rocks. Its dark green color provides a beautiful contrast to lighter stones like Dragon Stone.
source_categories: Aquatic Plants | Rhizome plants | Terrarium Plants

name: Anubias glabra
description: Anubias barteri var. glabra Care Guide Anubias glabra Care Guide Anubias barteri var. glabra (often sold as Anubias minima or Anubias lanceolata ) is a graceful and distinctive variant within the Anubias family. Unlike the common rounded leaves of most Anubias, the 'Glabra' variety is defined by its long, slender, lance-shaped (sword-like) leaves. Native to the rainforest streams of West Africa, it offers a refined verticality to aquascapes and is a favorite at Aquatic Exotica for customers looking for a hardy alternative to traditional bushy plants. Lighting Light Intensity: Prefers low to medium lighting . It is highly efficient in shaded conditions. High Light Warning: Avoid intense direct light. Its slow growth rate makes the long, narrow leaves prime targets for Green Spot Algae (GSA) . Placement: It is exceptionally versatile. Due to its upright growth, it works well in the midground or background, especially when tucked behind rocks to let the long leaves flow upward. Water Parameters Hardiness: Extremely robust; a true "beginner-friendly" plant that can adapt to almost any freshwater environment. Temperature: Prefers 72°F to 82°F (22°C – 28°C) . pH Range: 5.5 – 7.5. It thrives in slightly acidic to neutral water. Water Flow: Enjoys gentle to moderate water circulation. This mimics its natural habitat along riverbanks and keeps its glossy leaves free of debris. CO2 and Fertilization CO2: Not required. It will grow steadily in low-tech setups. However, CO2 injection will result in more robust foliage and a faster-creeping rhizome. Fertilization: Primarily an epiphytic feeder. It absorbs nutrients through its leaves and roots from the water column. Regular dosing with a liquid fertilizer is recommended to maintain its deep green luster. Planting and Substrate The Critical Rule: Never bury the rhizome. If the green horizontal stem is buried in soil or sand, it will rot, leading to the death of the plant. Attachment: Secure the plant to Driftwood, Lava Rock, or Slate using super glue gel or thread. Its roots are vigorous and will quickly anchor the plant to any porous surface. Vertical Interest: Because the leaves grow more vertically than other Anubias, it is an excellent choice for "green wall" effects on aquarium backgrounds. Growth and Appearance Leaf Shape: Narrow, lanceolate leaves with a smooth texture and slightly wavy edges. Size: Can grow taller than 'Nana' varieties, typically reaching 6 to 12 inches (15–30 cm) in height. Coloration: Displays a rich, dark green color that provides a cool, forest-like feel to the aquarium. Growth Rate: Slow to moderate. It is a stable plant that maintains its shape for long periods without needing frequent pruning. Propagation Rhizome Division: Easily propagated by cutting the rhizome with a sharp, clean blade. Requirement: Ensure each cutting has at least 3–4 healthy leaves and a few root filaments. Recovery: The mother plant will often sprout multiple new heads from the cut site, eventually creating a denser, more impressive colony. General Tips Cichlid and Perch Proof: Its tough, leathery leaves are generally ignored by herbivorous fish, making it a top choice for African Cichlid tanks. Clean-up Crew: Inhabitants like Amano shrimp or Nerite snails are great for keeping the long leaf blades free of biofilm. Aquatic Exotica Design: Use Anubias glabra alongside Anubias nana 'Petite' at the base to create a sense of depth and varied texture in your hardscape.
source_categories: Aquatic Plants | Rhizome plants | Terrarium Plants

name: Anubias 'Dragon Claw'
description: Anubias barteri 'Dragon Claw' Care Guide Anubias 'Dragon Claw' Care Guide Anubias 'Dragon Claw' is a unique and rare cultivar of the Anubias family, highly prized for its distinct leaf shape. Unlike the rounded or heart-shaped leaves of the standard Nana, 'Dragon Claw' features elongated, narrow, and slightly curved leaves that taper to a sharp point—resembling the talons of a dragon. Its dark green, leathery foliage and compact growth habit make it an exotic addition to any collection at Aquatic Exotica . Lighting Light Intensity: Prefers low to medium lighting . Like most Anubias, it is highly efficient at photosynthesizing in shaded areas. High Light Warning: Avoid placing it directly under high-intensity LED "hot spots." The slow growth rate of 'Dragon Claw' makes it a prime target for Green Spot Algae (GSA) if the lighting is too intense. Placement: It looks best when nestled into the midground, particularly in the shadows of driftwood or larger plants where its unique leaf silhouette can be highlighted. Water Parameters Hardiness: Extremely robust and adaptable to various water conditions, making it perfect for both beginners and experts. Temperature: Prefers 72°F to 82°F (22°C – 28°C) . pH Range: 6.0 – 7.8. It is very comfortable in the slightly acidic to neutral water common in most aquascapes. Water Flow: Enjoys moderate water circulation. This helps keep the "claw-like" leaves clean and ensures nutrient delivery to the rhizome. CO2 and Fertilization CO2: Not required for survival. However, CO2 injection will promote slightly faster growth and a more robust root system. Fertilization: It is an epiphytic feeder that draws nutrients primarily from the water column. Regular dosing of a comprehensive liquid fertilizer (NPK + Trace) is recommended. Indicator: If the sharp tips of the "claws" start to turn yellow, check for a potassium deficiency. Planting and Substrate The Critical Rule: Do not bury the rhizome. The horizontal stem must remain above the substrate. If it is buried under gravel or soil, the plant will suffer from rhizome rot. Attachment: Secure the plant to hardscape (Rocks, Wood, or Ceramic) using cyanoacrylate super glue gel or fishing line. Its roots are exceptionally strong and will eventually "grip" the surface like a claw. Aquascaping Tip: Its narrow leaves make it an excellent choice for "vertical" detailing on upright pieces of driftwood. Growth and Appearance Leaf Shape: Distinctly narrow, lanceolate, and often slightly curved or "hooked" at the tip. Size: Remains relatively compact, typically reaching 3–5 inches (7–12 cm) in height. Growth Rate: Very slow. It is a low-maintenance plant that stays in its designated spot without overtaking the tank. Propagation Rhizome Division: Propagate by cutting the rhizome into smaller sections with a sharp, sterile blade. Requirement: Each piece should have at least 3 healthy leaves to ensure it has enough energy to start new growth. Stimulation: Trimming the roots slightly when propagating can sometimes encourage the plant to anchor more quickly to new hardscape. General Tips Algae Control: Keep your phosphate levels balanced to prevent Green Spot Algae. Inhabitants like Amano shrimp or Nerite snails are great for keeping the "claws" polished. Durability: Its tough, leathery leaves are generally safe from herbivorous fish like Goldfish or Cichlids. Aquatic Exotica Design: Pair 'Dragon Claw' with Anubias Nana Gold or Bucephalandra Blue Curly to create a high-contrast texture and color map in your midground.
source_categories: Aquatic Plants | Rhizome plants | Terrarium Plants

name: Boston Fern
description: Boston Fern (Nephrolepis exaltata) Care Guide Boston Fern (Nephrolepis exaltata) Care Guide The Boston Fern ( Nephrolepis exaltata ) is a classic tropical plant known for its lush, arching, sword-shaped fronds. While it is one of the most popular air-purifying houseplants, its high demand for moisture and humidity makes it a staple for large terrariums and humid vivariums. In a home environment, it acts as a natural humidity regulator, though it can be a bit of a "shedder" if its needs aren't met. Lighting Boston Ferns thrive in bright, indirect sunlight . Direct Sun: Harsh, direct afternoon sun will quickly scorch the delicate leaflets, leading to yellowing or "crispy" brown fronds. Low Light: While they can survive in lower light, they will become "leggy" and sparse, losing their signature bushy density. Filtered morning light near an East-facing window is usually the "sweet spot." Watering and Humidity Humidity: This is the most critical factor. They prefer 60-80% humidity . In dry indoor air, the tips of the leaves will turn brown and fall off, creating a mess. Watering: The soil should be kept consistently moist but never waterlogged. They do not tolerate drying out; if the soil feels dry to the touch, it is time to water. Water Quality: They are sensitive to heavily chlorinated tap water. Using distilled water or rainwater is recommended to keep the foliage vibrant green. Temperature Ideal temperature range: 65-75°F (18-24°C) . They are tropical plants and should be protected from cold drafts, air conditioning vents, and temperatures below 50°F (10°C). Sudden temperature fluctuations can cause significant leaf drop. Substrate and Fertilization Substrate: A loose, well-draining potting mix rich in organic matter is best. A blend of peat moss or coco coir with a bit of perlite and compost works well. Fertilizer: During the growing season (spring/summer), feed every 4-6 weeks with a balanced liquid fertilizer diluted to half-strength . Avoid feeding during the winter when growth naturally slows. Terrarium Use and Varieties Growth Habit: Standard Boston Ferns can grow quite large (up to 3 feet wide), making them better suited for large vivariums or as room plants. Compact Varieties: For smaller terrariums, look for: 'Duffy' (Lemon Button Fern): A much smaller relative with round, button-like leaves. 'Fluffy Ruffles': A smaller, more upright variety with stiff, multi-divided leaves. 'Dallas': Specifically bred to stay compact and tolerate lower light and lower humidity. Maintenance and Propagation Pruning: Regularly trim away brown or dead fronds at the base to encourage fresh, green growth and improve airflow. Division: The easiest way to propagate is by division . During repotting, you can gently pull the root ball apart into smaller sections (ensuring each has roots and fronds) and plant them separately. Runners: They often produce long, leafless "runners" (stolons) that can be pinned to the soil to grow new plantlets. General Tips The "Shedding": Don't panic if your fern drops some leaves when you first bring it home; it is likely adjusting to the change in humidity. Pet Safety: Boston Ferns are non-toxic to cats and dogs, making them a safe choice for pet owners. Cleaning: Use a gentle spray bottle to mist the leaves; this both increases humidity and keeps the fronds free of dust.
source_categories: Indoor Plants | Terrarium Plants

name: Syngonium Tiffany
description: Syngonium Tiffany Care Guide Syngonium Tiffany Care Guide Syngonium Tiffany (Syngonium podophyllum 'Tiffany') is a stunning variegated climbing vine native to Central and South America. This captivating variety is prized for its arrow-shaped leaves that display beautiful cream, white, and green variegation with subtle pink undertones. As the plant matures, it transitions from compact juvenile leaves to larger, lobed adult foliage when provided with climbing support. The high variegation makes this cultivar particularly sought after among houseplant enthusiasts. Lighting Syngonium Tiffany requires bright, indirect light to maintain its striking variegation. Higher light levels enhance the cream and white markings while preventing reversion to green. Can tolerate medium light but variegation may fade and growth may slow significantly. Avoid direct sunlight as it can scorch the delicate variegated leaves and cause browning. Water Parameters Water when the top 1-2 inches of soil feel dry, typically every 7-10 days in growing season. Maintain consistent moisture but avoid waterlogged conditions that can cause root rot. Use filtered or distilled water when possible as variegated plants can be sensitive to chemicals. Reduce watering frequency in winter when growth naturally slows down. Water thoroughly until excess drains from drainage holes, then empty saucers. Yellow leaves typically indicate overwatering while crispy edges suggest underwatering. CO2 and Fertilization CO2 supplementation is not necessary for this houseplant. Feed every 2-4 weeks during spring and summer with balanced liquid fertilizer. Use diluted fertilizer (half strength) to prevent burning the sensitive variegated foliage. Reduce feeding to monthly or stop completely during fall and winter dormancy. Planting and Substrate Requires well-draining, airy potting mix to prevent root rot and promote healthy growth. Ideal soil blend: 40% potting soil, 20% perlite, 20% orchid bark, 20% coconut coir. Choose pots with adequate drainage holes to prevent water accumulation. Repot annually in spring, increasing pot size by 1-2 inches in diameter. Growth and Appearance Syngonium Tiffany is a fast-growing climbing vine when provided with support. Features arrow-shaped juvenile leaves with cream, white, and green variegation. Mature leaves become larger and more lobed when the plant is allowed to climb. Can reach 3-6 feet indoors with proper support and care. High variegation makes this cultivar more light-sensitive than solid green varieties. Pink undertones may appear in optimal lighting conditions. Propagation Stem cuttings root easily in water or moist potting mix. Cut 4-6 inch stems with at least 2-3 nodes for best success rates. Remove lower leaves and place cuttings in water or directly into moist soil. Water propagation typically shows roots in 2-4 weeks under bright, indirect light. General Tips Syngonium Tiffany requires more attention than solid green varieties due to high variegation. Provide moss poles or trellises to encourage climbing and larger leaf development. Maintain humidity levels of 50-60% to prevent brown leaf edges and promote health. Pinch growing tips regularly to encourage bushier, more compact growth. The plant is toxic to pets and children due to calcium oxalate crystals. Temperature range of 65-80°F (18-27°C) is ideal for optimal growth and variegation.
source_categories: Indoor Plants | Terrarium Plants

name: Peperomia Green Creeper
description: Peperomia Green Creeper Care Guide Peperomia "Green Creeper" Care Guide The Peperomia "Green Creeper" is a popular and relatively easy-to-care-for houseplant known for its attractive green foliage and vining habit. It’s a delightful addition to any indoor space, whether as a centerpiece on a table or hanging from a basket. Light Peperomia "Green Creeper" thrives in bright, indirect light. Place it near an east or west-facing window, ensuring it's protected from the harsh afternoon sun, which can scorch the leaves. While it can tolerate lower light, reduce watering frequency in shadier spots. Morning sun is beneficial, but avoid direct afternoon sun. Watering As a semi-succulent, this plant doesn't need frequent watering. Water only when the soil has dried out after the previous watering. Overwatering is detrimental and can lead to root rot and leaf drop, {Link: according to Ugaoo https://www.ugaoo.com/products/peperomia-green-creeper-plant} and {Link: YouTube https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=RgCFaXlRxPw}. Ensure the pot has good drainage holes to allow excess water to escape. Allow the soil to dry slightly between waterings, but don't let it stay dry for too long, as underwatering can also cause leaves to drop. Humidity Peperomia "Green Creeper" prefers moderate to high humidity levels. Mist the plant occasionally or place it near a humidifier, {Link: says Ugaoo https://www.ugaoo.com/products/peperomia-green-creeper-plant}. A humidity level of 50% is generally good, but misting can help keep the leaves healthy and dust-free. Temperature The ideal temperature range is between 65-75°F (18-24°C). Protect the plant from cold drafts and sudden temperature fluctuations, as these can stress the plant. Soil Use a well-draining, loose soil mix. A mix containing potting soil, perlite, and potentially some cow manure or peat moss is recommended for optimal drainage and moisture retention. Avoid soil mixes that are excessively heavy with coco peat or manure. Fertilization Feed your plant with a balanced liquid fertilizer diluted to half strength every 4-6 weeks during the growing season (spring and summer). Alternatively, apply a well-balanced liquid fertilizer once every three months, {Link: according to Plan A Plant https://planaplant.com/products/peperomia-green-creeper-set-of-5}. You can also use vermicompost weekly and seaweed monthly for greener leaves, along with Epsom salt monthly for better blooming. Pruning and Maintenance Pruning can encourage the plant to become bushier. Regularly remove any yellowing or decaying leaves. Propagation Peperomia "Green Creeper" is easy to propagate from stem or leaf cuttings. Simply snip a healthy piece, let it dry for a few hours (optional), and then place it in water or moist soil. Roots should develop within a few weeks. When propagating in soil, ensure it's a good quality mix, potentially with added perlite, {Link: says a YouTube video https://m.youtube.com/watch?v=RKY5L6_dDrY&pp=ygULI3Byb3BvZ2F0aW4%3D}. Covering the pot with cling film or placing it in a sealed bag can help retain humidity and speed up the process. Common Problems **Yellow and floppy leaves:** Often a sign of overwatering. Water only when the soil is dry to the touch. **Brownish and dehydrated leaves:** May indicate excessive heat and lack of humidity. **Limp leaves:** Can be caused by either overwatering or underwatering. **Leggy growth or root rot:** Common problems due to poor light or overwatering. Requires proper watering, light optimization, and regular pruning. **Droopy leaves, mushy stems, and moldy soil:** Strong indicators of overwatering. **Pests:** Like many houseplants, Peperomia can be susceptible to mealybugs, spider mites, and aphids.
source_categories: Indoor Plants | Terrarium Plants

name: Ficus pumila 'Minima'
description: Ficus pumila 'Minima' (Oak Leaf Creeping Fig) Care Guide Ficus pumila 'Minima' Care Guide Ficus pumila 'Minima' , commonly known as the Oak Leaf Creeping Fig or Miniature Creeping Fig, is a tiny-leaved, vigorous climber and groundcover. It is a miniaturized version of the standard Creeping Fig and is highly prized in the Aquatic Exotica collection for its ability to "carpet" terrarium walls and hardscape with dense, lacy foliage. It is a versatile plant that creates a lush, ancient forest look in any enclosed environment. Lighting Light Intensity: Thrives in bright, indirect light . Low Light: It can tolerate medium light, but its growth rate will slow significantly, and the stems may become "leggy". Direct Sun: Avoid harsh, direct afternoon sun, which can quickly scorch and dry out the delicate, paper-thin leaves. Watering and Humidity Humidity: Requires high humidity (60-90%) to prevent the leaves from becoming crispy. It is an ideal plant for closed terrariums and vivariums. Watering: Keep the substrate consistently moist but not waterlogged. Ficus pumila does not tolerate drying out; if the soil dries completely, the plant may drop its leaves rapidly. Misting: Regular misting is beneficial, especially when the plant is being trained to climb background walls or hardscape. Temperature Ideal temperature range: 65°F to 80°F (18°C – 27°C) . It is sensitive to frost and very cold drafts. When kept in indoor terrariums, normal room temperatures are perfectly suitable. Substrate and Fertilization Substrate: Prefers a well-draining, nutrient-rich soil mix. A blend of peat moss, coco coir, and perlite works well to retain moisture while providing drainage. Fertilizer: Apply a balanced liquid fertilizer diluted to half-strength every 4–6 weeks during the growing season (spring and summer). Growth and Appearance Growth Habit: A fast-growing climber that uses aerial roots to "glue" itself to surfaces. It can also be used as a spreading groundcover. Foliage: Tiny, heart-shaped or "oak-like" leaves that are much smaller than the standard variety. Maintenance: Because it is a vigorous grower, it requires regular pruning to keep it from overtaking other smaller plants in your setup. Propagation Extremely easy to propagate via stem cuttings . Snip a section of the stem and place it on moist soil or in water. In a high-humidity environment, roots will typically form at the nodes within a few weeks. General Tips Terrarium Use: Perfect for covering 3D backgrounds, driftwood, or stones. Pet Safety: Caution: Like most Ficus species, the sap can be a mild irritant to skin and is toxic if ingested by pets. Use care when pruning. Aquatic Exotica Design: Pair Ficus pumila 'Minima' with larger-leaved plants like Peperomia 'Rosso' to create a beautiful contrast in scale and texture.
source_categories: Exotic Plants | Premium | Terrarium Plants

name: Peperomia Raisinet
description: Peperomia Raisinet Care Guide Peperomia Raisinet Care Guide Peperomia Raisinet (Peperomia clusiifolia 'Red Edge') is a striking succulent-like houseplant native to the Caribbean and South America. This compact variety is prized for its thick, waxy leaves that feature beautiful cream and green variegation with distinctive red or pink edges. The leaves are spoon-shaped and arranged in a rosette pattern, creating an attractive, sculptural appearance that makes it popular among collectors. Lighting Peperomia Raisinet thrives in bright, indirect light conditions. Bright light enhances the red edge coloration and maintains compact growth. Can tolerate medium light but red edges may fade and growth may slow. Avoid direct sunlight as it can scorch the thick leaves and cause burning. Water Parameters Water when the top 2 inches of soil are completely dry, typically every 10-14 days. Peperomia Raisinet stores water in its thick, succulent-like leaves making it very drought-tolerant. Water thoroughly but allow excess water to drain completely to prevent root rot. Reduce watering significantly in winter when growth slows down. Use room temperature water and avoid getting water on the leaves. Overwatering is more harmful than underwatering for this species. CO2 and Fertilization CO2 supplementation is not necessary for this houseplant. Feed monthly during spring and summer with diluted liquid fertilizer. Use balanced fertilizer (10-10-10) diluted to quarter strength to prevent burning. Reduce feeding to every 2-3 months during fall and winter dormancy. Planting and Substrate Requires very well-draining, lightweight potting mix similar to cactus soil. Ideal soil blend: 40% potting soil, 30% perlite, 20% coarse sand, 10% orchid bark. Choose pots with multiple drainage holes; terracotta pots are excellent for moisture control. Shallow, wide pots work well as it has a compact, spreading growth habit. Growth and Appearance Peperomia Raisinet is a slow-growing, compact succulent-type plant. Features thick, waxy spoon-shaped leaves with cream and green variegation. Distinctive red or pink edges on leaves that intensify with proper lighting. Grows in a rosette pattern reaching 6-8 inches in height and spread. Produces small, inconspicuous flower spikes on mature, healthy plants. Leaves may develop more intense red coloration under slight stress conditions. Propagation Leaf cuttings are the most reliable propagation method. Cut healthy leaves at the base including a small portion of stem. Allow cut surfaces to callus for 24-48 hours before planting. Root in well-draining cactus mix under bright, indirect light. General Tips Peperomia Raisinet is very easy to care for and extremely drought-tolerant. Sensitive to overwatering, so water sparingly and ensure good drainage. Remove any soft, mushy, or yellowing leaves immediately to prevent rot. Clean leaves occasionally with a damp cloth to maintain their waxy appearance. The plant is non-toxic to pets and children, making it household safe. Temperature range of 65-80°F (18-27°C) is ideal for optimal growth.
source_categories: Indoor Plants | Terrarium Plants

name: Cryptanthus "Pink Starlight"
description: Cryptanthus bivittatus "Pink Starlite" Care Guide Cryptanthus bivittatus "Pink Starlite" Care Guide Cryptanthus bivittatus "Pink Starlite" (often spelled "Pink Starlight") is a stunning terrestrial bromeliad native to the rainforest floors of Brazil. Unlike many bromeliads that grow on trees (epiphytes), the Earth Star grows directly in the soil. It is famous for its stemless, star-shaped rosette of wavy leaves that feature vibrant bubblegum-pink margins and deep forest-green centers. This "Drama Queen" of the bromeliad world is a functional powerhouse, known for its ability to purify indoor air by removing toxic chemicals and increasing oxygen levels at night. Lighting Light Intensity: Requires bright, indirect light to maintain its intense pink coloration. The brighter the light (without direct sun), the more the foliage will "sparkle" like starlight. Low Light: In low light, the vibrant pink and red tones will fade to a dull, light green, and the plant may lose its compact star shape. Warning: Avoid direct, hot sun. Harsh sunlight will scorch the leaves or cause "bleaching," where the pink turns a pale, leathery white. Watering and Humidity Watering Technique: Unlike other bromeliads, do not pour water into the central rosette (the cup). Water trapped there can cause mildew, fungal growth, or crown rot. Always water the soil directly. Frequency: Keep the soil consistently moist but not soggy. Water approximately once a week when the top inch of soil feels dry. In winter, reduce watering to every 10–20 days as growth slows. Water Quality: Use distilled water, RO water, or rainwater at room temperature. Earth Stars are sensitive to chlorine and fluoride in tap water, which can cause brown leaf tips. Humidity: Thrives in high humidity (50-70%). Mist the plant every couple of days or place it on a pebble tray. It is an ideal candidate for closed terrariums and humid bathrooms. Temperature Ideal temperature range: 60°F to 85°F (15°C – 29°C) . The "Pink Starlite" is not cold-tolerant. Keep it away from air conditioning vents, cold drafts, or heating units that dry out the air. Protect it from freezing temperatures; exposure to frost is usually fatal. Soil and Fertilization Substrate: Prefers a rich, acidic, and well-draining medium. A commercial peat-based potting soil or an "African Violet" mix is ideal. DIY Mix: Combine equal parts potting soil, peat moss, and sand or perlite for optimal drainage and moisture retention. Fertilizer: Feed sparingly. Apply a balanced liquid fertilizer diluted to 1/4 strength once a month during spring and summer. Tip: Spray the diluted fertilizer onto the leaves (foliar feeding) rather than the soil to enhance color, but avoid the central cup. Growth and Appearance Size: A small, slow-growing perennial. It typically reaches 3–5 inches in height with a spread of 6–10 inches. Foliage: Stiff, lance-shaped leaves with finely serrated (spiny) edges. handle with care as the tiny "teeth" can be sharp. Flowers: Produces small, inconspicuous white flowers in the center of the rosette. Like all bromeliads, the mother plant will slowly die after flowering, directing its energy into "pups." Propagation Propagation is easily done via offsets or "pups" that appear at the base or between the leaves after blooming. Wait until the pup is at least 1/3 the size of the mother plant. Gently tug or snip the pup off and press it into moist soil. Covering the new pot with a plastic bag can help lock in the high humidity needed for the new roots to establish. General Tips Pet Safety: Non-toxic to cats and dogs according to the ASPCA. However, keep it out of reach to avoid pets being poked by the serrated leaf margins. Air Purification: A "star" at cleaning the air, this plant filters out pollutants like formaldehyde and benzene. Maintenance: Prune dead leaves at the base with clean scissors to maintain a tidy appearance and prevent pests like mealybugs from hiding in debris.
source_categories: Indoor Plants | Terrarium Plants

name: Fittonia 'Pink'
description: Fittonia albivenis 'Pink' (Nerve Plant) Care Guide Fittonia 'Pink' (Nerve Plant) Care Guide Fittonia 'Pink' , commonly known as the Pink Nerve Plant or Mosaic Plant , is a striking tropical perennial from South America. It is a standout in the terrarium hobby, like the one shown in your image, because of its vibrant, high-contrast pink veins against olive-green leaves. These plants are widely known as "Drama Queens" because they will dramatically wilt (faint) when thirsty, only to fully recover within hours of being watered. Lighting Fittonia 'Pink' thrives in bright, indirect light . Direct Sun: Strictly avoid direct sunlight; the thin leaves are highly susceptible to scorching or bleaching. Low Light: In low light, the plant may become "leggy" as it stretches for light, and the vibrant pink coloration of the veins can fade. Watering and Humidity Humidity: Requires high humidity (60-90%) to keep leaf edges from turning brown and crisp. It is ideally suited for closed terrariums. Watering: Keep the substrate consistently moist but not waterlogged. They do not tolerate drying out between waterings. Water Quality: Using room-temperature distilled or rainwater helps prevent leaf tip burn caused by chemicals in tap water. Temperature Ideal temperature range: 65°F to 80°F (18°C – 27°C) . As a tropical species, it is highly sensitive to cold drafts and temperatures below 55°F (13°C). Substrate and Fertilization Substrate: Use a well-draining, moisture-retentive mix like peat moss or coco coir blended with perlite. Fertilizer: During the growing season, apply a balanced liquid fertilizer diluted to half-strength every 4–6 weeks. Growth and Appearance Growth Habit: A low-growing, spreading plant that rarely exceeds 6 inches in height. Foliage: Features intricate, lace-like pink veins. Varieties like 'Frankie' may display even more dominant pink hues across the entire leaf. Propagation Fittonias are very easy to propagate via stem cuttings . Take a cutting with at least two nodes and place it in water or moist soil; roots typically appear within 1–2 weeks in high humidity. General Tips Pruning: Regularly pinch off the tips of the stems to encourage a bushier, denser growth habit. Flowers: It may produce small, upright flower spikes. Many growers snip these off to keep the plant's energy focused on its foliage. Terrarium Companion: As seen in your image, it pairs perfectly with mosses and small Bucephalandra , as they share identical high-humidity requirements.
source_categories: Indoor Plants | Terrarium Plants

name: Pilea nummulariifolia (Creeping Charlie 3 Cuttings)
description: Pilea nummulariifolia (Creeping Charlie) Care Guide Pilea nummulariifolia (Creeping Charlie) Care Guide Pilea nummulariifolia, commonly known as "Creeping Charlie," is a lush, perennial evergreen creeper native to the Caribbean and northern South America. It is beloved for its bright green, crinkled leaves that grow along trailing reddish stems, making it a popular choice for hanging baskets, terrariums, and as a ground cover in tropical vivariums. Here's a detailed guide to help you care for Pilea nummulariifolia: Lighting Pilea nummulariifolia thrives in bright, indirect light to partial shade. Direct sunlight should be avoided as it can easily scorch the delicate, textured leaves, leading to brown patches. In a terrarium or vivarium setting, it does well under standard full-spectrum LED lighting. If the light is too low, the plant will become leggy with smaller leaves and longer stems. Watering and Humidity This plant loves moisture and should be kept consistently damp but not soggy. Ideal humidity: 50-80%. {Link: According to www.glass-box-tropicals.com https://www.glassboxtropicals.com/pilea-nummulariifolia-creeping-charlie/}, it is an excellent candidate for humid enclosures and thrives when the air is moist. Water when the surface of the soil just begins to feel dry. It does not tolerate prolonged drought, which causes the leaves to wilt and drop. In household environments, regular misting or a pebble tray can help maintain the necessary humidity levels. Temperature Ideal temperature: 65-80°F (18-27°C). As a tropical plant, it is not cold-hardy and should be kept away from drafts or temperatures below 55°F (13°C). It enjoys the stable, warm temperatures typically found inside homes or enclosed glass tanks. Soil and Fertilization Use a well-draining potting mix rich in organic matter. A blend of peat moss, perlite, and standard potting soil works well. In terrariums, it grows easily in ABG (Atlanta Botanical Gardens) mix or on damp sphagnum moss. Feed every 4-6 weeks during the spring and summer growing season with a balanced liquid fertilizer diluted to half strength. Growth and Appearance Pilea nummulariifolia is a fast-growing, trailing vine that can quickly form a dense mat. The leaves are round (nummular), deeply veined, and have a puckered or "quilted" texture that adds significant visual interest. The stems are slightly fleshy and can root wherever a node touches moist soil, allowing it to "creep" across surfaces effectively. It can be used as a cascading plant in hanging pots or as a lush green background/carpet in a vivarium. Propagation Propagation is incredibly simple through stem cuttings. Take a 3-4 inch cutting that includes at least two nodes and place it in water or directly into moist soil or moss. Roots usually form within a few days to a week. Once a root system is established, the plant will begin to produce new foliage rapidly. General Tips Regular pruning is often necessary to keep the plant contained, especially in smaller terrariums where it can outcompete other species. Ensure good air circulation if humidity is very high to prevent fungal issues or leaf rot. Pilea nummulariifolia is generally non-toxic to cats and dogs, making it a safe choice for pet-friendly homes. If the plant becomes too thin or leggy at the top, pinch back the growing tips to encourage a bushier growth habit.
source_categories: Indoor Plants | Terrarium Plants

name: Bucephalandra Black Cobra (3 Rhizomes)
description: Bucephalandra "Black Cobra" Care Guide Bucephalandra "Black Cobra" Care Guide Bucephalandra "Black Cobra" is a sought-after variety of Bucephalandra known for its distinctive dark, sometimes almost black, coloration and unique leaf shape. It is a slow-growing plant that adds a unique touch to planted aquariums. Here's a guide to help you care for Bucephalandra "Black Cobra": Lighting Bucephalandra "Black Cobra" can thrive in low to medium lighting conditions. While low light is sufficient for survival, moderate to higher lighting can help bring out its best coloration and encourage faster growth. If using higher light levels, be cautious about excessive algae growth, as Bucephalandra is a slow-growing plant and can be prone to it. Water Parameters Bucephalandra "Black Cobra", like other Bucephalandra species, prefers stable water conditions. Ideal temperature: 71-79°F (21-26°C). pH range: 6.0-8.0. Some sources suggest a slightly narrower range of 6.0-7.5. Good water flow is beneficial, mimicking its natural habitat in streams with moderate to high flow. Clean, well-filtered water with regular water changes will help prevent melting and ensure healthy growth. Maintain some water hardness (GH/KH), with a dGH of 5 or above being helpful. CO2 and Fertilization CO2 injection is not strictly necessary but can promote faster growth and enhance coloration. Regular fertilization with a comprehensive liquid fertilizer will provide essential nutrients. Bucephalandra primarily absorbs nutrients from the water column. Planting and Substrate Bucephalandra "Black Cobra" is an epiphytic plant and should not have its rhizome buried in the substrate, as this can lead to rot. Attach it to hardscape, such as rocks or driftwood, using super glue (gel type is recommended) or thread. If you choose to plant it in the substrate, ensure the roots are covered but the rhizome remains exposed. Growth and Appearance Bucephalandra "Black Cobra" is a slow-growing plant. It typically features medium-sized leaves (4-6cm) with a rounded ovate shape. In its submerged form, the leaves can exhibit a metallic dark purple color, although achieving the best coloration may require optimal conditions, including high-power RGB lighting and CO2 injection. Younger leaves may initially appear green and gradually darken over time, potentially developing an almost black color with bluish iridescence under the right conditions. Propagation Propagate by cutting the rhizome into sections, each with a few leaves and roots. Reattach these sections to hardscape as you would with a new plant. General Tips Bucephalandra "Black Cobra" is considered an easy plant to care for, even for beginners. Maintain stable water parameters to prevent melting and rotting. Melting can occur when the plant adjusts to a new environment, but it will usually recover and produce new growth from the rhizome if the rhizome remains healthy. Regularly remove any unhealthy leaves to encourage new growth. Good water flow is beneficial for promoting healthy growth.
source_categories: Exotic Plants | Rhizome plants

name: Bucephalandra Godzilla (Rare, One rhizome)
description: Bucephalandra "Godzilla" Care Guide Bucephalandra "Godzilla" Care Guide Bucephalandra "Godzilla" is a popular and visually striking variety known for its unique leaf texture and robust growth. Originating from Borneo, it is characterized by its long, narrow leaves with highly ruffled or "crinkled" edges, giving it a prehistoric and dramatic appearance that stands out in any aquascape. Here's a detailed guide to help you care for Bucephalandra "Godzilla": Lighting Bucephalandra "Godzilla" is adaptable and can thrive in low to medium-high lighting. Under medium to high light, the plant will develop more intense coloration, often displaying deep greens, blues, and even purple hues. To prevent algae from attaching to its textured, slow-growing leaves, a moderate PAR level of 40-60 umols is generally recommended. Water Parameters Stability is crucial for "Godzilla" to prevent the rhizome from melting during acclimation. Ideal temperature: 70-80°F (21-27°C). {Link: According to www.buceplant.com https://buceplant.com/products/bucephalandra-godzilla}, maintaining a stable tropical temperature is essential for long-term health. pH range: 6.0-7.5. It prefers slightly acidic water but is tolerant of a wider range if kept stable. This variety thrives in areas of the tank with high water flow, which helps deliver nutrients and prevents organic waste from settling on the ruffled leaves. A dGH of 5 or higher is beneficial for maintaining the structural integrity of its thick leaves. CO2 and Fertilization While "Godzilla" can be grown without CO2, supplementation will result in much faster growth and more pronounced "waves" on the leaf margins. It is a heavy feeder from the water column; regular dosing of a comprehensive liquid fertilizer is necessary. Iron and trace element supplementation is particularly helpful for enhancing the iridescent sheen that makes this variety so attractive. Planting and Substrate Bucephalandra "Godzilla" is an epiphytic plant and must not have its rhizome buried in the substrate. Burying the rhizome will lead to anaerobic rot and the eventual death of the plant. It should be attached to hardscape, such as lava rocks, dragon stone, or driftwood, using aquarium-safe super glue gel or dark thread. Its root system is quite strong once established and will grip tightly to porous surfaces. Growth and Appearance Bucephalandra "Godzilla" is a slow to moderate grower compared to other Buce varieties. The leaves are long, narrow, and feature extremely wavy or ruffled edges that give the plant a "spiky" look. Mature leaves are typically a dark, forest green with a bluish iridescence, while new growth can appear reddish or bronze. The leaves are covered in tiny silver spots (stomata) that are clearly visible under good lighting. Propagation Propagation is straightforward: simply divide the rhizome using sharp, sterilized scissors or a blade. Each divided section should have at least 2-3 leaves and a portion of the rhizome to ensure successful regrowth. Once cut, the new sections can be glued or tied to different areas of the hardscape. General Tips Because of its ruffled leaves, "Godzilla" is a magnet for detritus; regular turkey basting or high flow helps keep it clean. If the plant experiences "melt" upon introduction, remove the decaying leaves but leave the rhizome alone, as it will likely sprout new, adapted growth. Algae-eating inhabitants like Amano shrimp or Otocinclus are highly recommended to keep the slow-growing leaves pristine. It is a versatile plant that works exceptionally well as a focal point on midground wood or tucked into crevices between rocks. Can be grown emersed in high-humidity environments, though the leaf ruffles may be less pronounced than in the submerged form.

name: Bucephalandra Red Velvet (3 Rhizomes)
description: Bucephalandra "Red Velvet" Care Guide Bucephalandra "Red Velvet" Care Guide Bucephalandra "Red Velvet" is a captivating and highly sought-after variety of Bucephalandra, known for its deep red or maroon coloration and velvety leaf texture. Native to Borneo, these slow-growing plants add a vibrant and unique element to planted aquariums. Lighting Bucephalandra "Red Velvet" can thrive in low to medium lighting conditions. Moderate lighting is recommended to bring out its best colors and encourage healthy growth. While it can tolerate higher lighting, be mindful of algae growth, as Bucephalandra are slow growers. Excessive light can cause leaves to burn and stress the plant, especially without adequate CO2 and nutrients. Water Parameters Bucephalandra "Red Velvet" prefers stable water conditions. Ideal temperature range: 70-82°F (21-28°C) or {Link: 71-82.5°F (21.5-28°C) according to Shrimp and Snail Breeder https://aquariumbreeder.com/bucephalandra-care-guide-planting-growing-and-propagation/}. pH range: 6.0-7.5. Good water flow is beneficial, mimicking its natural habitat in streams with moderate to high flow. Maintain clean, well-filtered water with regular water changes to prevent the buildup of waste products. Some hardness (GH/KH) is helpful, with a dGH of 5 or above being recommended. CO2 and Fertilization CO2 injection is not essential for survival, but it will significantly enhance growth and coloration. Bucephalandra primarily absorbs nutrients from the water column, so a liquid fertilizer is beneficial. Regular fertilization with a comprehensive liquid fertilizer containing N, P, K, and micronutrients like iron and magnesium, will support healthy growth and vibrant colors. Planting and Substrate Bucephalandra "Red Velvet" is an epiphytic plant and should not have its rhizome buried in the substrate, as this can lead to rot and melting. Attach it to hardscape elements like rocks or driftwood using super glue (gel type recommended) or thread. If planting in the substrate, ensure only the roots are covered and the rhizome remains exposed. Growth and Appearance Bucephalandra "Red Velvet" is a slow-growing plant. It features deep red or maroon leaves with a velvety texture. The leaves are generally 4-6 cm long and 2-4 cm wide, often with wavy edges. Young leaves may be a lighter color, deepening to a rich red or maroon as they mature. It can reach a height of 10-15 cm (4-6 inches). Some varieties may also show iridescent qualities, reflecting blue or purple hues. Propagation Propagate by cutting the rhizome into sections, each with a few leaves and roots. Reattach these sections to hardscape or plant them (with the rhizome exposed) in the substrate. Bare rhizomes can also produce new shoots if healthy and provided with good conditions. General Tips Bucephalandra "Red Velvet" is relatively hardy and considered easy to care for, even for beginners. However, it is sensitive to drastic changes in water parameters, so maintaining stability is key. Melting can occur when introduced to a new environment or with unstable parameters, but new growth will typically emerge from a healthy rhizome. Regularly remove any yellowing, decaying, or melting leaves. Good water flow is crucial for distributing nutrients and preventing detritus buildup. ```

name: Pothos scandens (One Plant)
description: Pothos scandens Care Guide Pothos scandens Care Guide Pothos scandens , often called the "Climbing Aroid," is the true Pothos species. While most common houseplants sold as "Pothos" are actually Epipremnum aureum , Pothos scandens is a distinct species native to the tropical forests of the Indian Subcontinent and Southeast Asia. It is famous for its unique leaf structure, where the petiole (leaf stem) is flattened and winged, making the foliage look like two leaves joined together—a look similar to Kaffir lime leaves. This resilient climber is highly valued in Ayurvedic medicine for its cooling properties and is a fantastic choice for collectors looking for a "shingling" or climbing vine that is both rare and easy to care for. Lighting Light Intensity: Thrives in bright, indirect light . It can tolerate medium to low light conditions, but its growth will slow down significantly. Direct Sun: Strictly avoid intense afternoon sun, which will scorch and brown the delicate foliage. Indoor Placement: An east- or west-facing window is ideal. It also performs exceptionally well under artificial grow lights in office or terrarium settings. Watering and Humidity Watering Frequency: Water when the top 50% of the soil is dry. It generally enjoys weekly watering sessions during the spring and summer. Watering Technique: Ensure the soil stays moist but never soggy. Overwatering can quickly lead to root decay and yellowing leaves. Humidity: Prefers high humidity between 60-70% . While it can adapt to normal room humidity, it will grow much faster and produce larger leaves if misted every couple of days or kept near a humidifier. Water Quality: Using distilled or rainwater is best to prevent mineral buildup on its unique leaf surfaces. Temperature Ideal temperature range: 68°F to 95°F (20°C – 35°C) . It is native to wet tropical biomes and prefers warm, stable conditions. Cold Protection: Protect it from temperatures below 50°F (10°C) and avoid cold drafts from air conditioning units. Soil and Fertilization Substrate: Needs a well-draining, airy soil mix with high organic matter. A quality indoor potting mix blended with peat moss, leaf mold, or orchid bark works perfectly. Fertilizer: Apply a well-diluted balanced liquid feed every 3 to 4 weeks during the growing season. Winter Care: Reduce fertilization and watering in the winter when the plant enters a slower growth phase. Growth and Appearance Growth Habit: A vigorous climber that uses aerial roots to "stick" to tree trunks or walls. It can reach lengths of 10-12 feet indoors if given a support to climb. Leaf Structure: Features dark green, glossy leaves with a distinctive "winged" petiole. In its native habitat, it can grow in a shingling pattern (leaves flat against a surface). Flowers: Occasionally produces small, globular, off-white flowers, though these are rare in an indoor environment. Propagation Stem Cuttings: Propagation is straightforward via stem cuttings. Take a 5-inch cutting with at least two or three nodes. Method: You can root the cutting in water or directly in moist soil. If using soil, keeping the cutting in a high-humidity environment (like a plastic bag) will speed up root development. General Tips Air Purification: Like its cousins, Pothos scandens is excellent at removing toxic chemicals from the air and increasing oxygen levels. Pet Safety: Toxic to cats and dogs. It contains calcium oxalate crystals which cause irritation and vomiting if ingested. Keep out of reach of pets and children. Mounting: To see this plant’s true potential, mount it on a moss pole or a cork bark slab. This encourages the leaves to maintain their unique shingling habit. Maintenance: Clean the leaves regularly with a damp cloth to enhance their appearance and prevent pests like spider mites or mealybugs.
source_categories: Terrarium Plants

name: Peperomia 'Rosso'
description: Peperomia caperata 'Rosso' Care Guide Peperomia 'Rosso' (Emerald Ripple Peperomia) Care Guide Peperomia 'Rosso' is a stunning, compact cultivar of Peperomia caperata . It is a sport of the popular 'Luna Red' and is highly prized for its dramatic color contrast: deep, glossy green on the top of the leaf and a vibrant, earthy red on the underside. Its pointed, lance-shaped leaves grow in a tight, symmetrical rosette, making it a perfect "jewel" plant for tabletops, desks, and small terrariums. Lighting Light Intensity: Prefers bright, indirect light . This is essential for maintaining the deep red color on the leaf undersides. Direct Sun: Avoid harsh, direct afternoon sun, which can cause the colors to fade or burn the fleshy leaves. Low Light: While it can tolerate medium light, it may become "leggy" as the stems stretch toward the light source, and the foliage will lose its compact rosette shape. Artificial Light: It performs exceptionally well under fluorescent or LED office lights. Watering and Humidity Watering Technique: Like a semi-succulent, it stores water in its thick leaves. Water only when the top 50-75% of the soil is dry. Bottom Watering: It is highly recommended to water from the bottom or avoid the crown. Getting water trapped in the center of the rosette can lead to rot. Humidity: Prefers moderate to high humidity (40-60%). While it handles average home air better than most tropicals, it will thrive in a terrarium or on a pebble tray. Warning: Overwatering is the most common cause of death for Peperomia 'Rosso'. If the leaves feel soft or "mushy," stop watering immediately. Temperature Ideal temperature range: 65°F to 75°F (18°C – 24°C) . It is sensitive to sudden temperature drops. Keep it away from cold drafts, air conditioning vents, and heaters. It is not frost-tolerant and should never be exposed to temperatures below 50°F (10°C). Soil and Fertilization Substrate: Requires a very well-draining, "chunky" mix. A blend of high-quality potting soil with plenty of perlite, orchid bark, or pumice is ideal. Potting: Peperomias have small root systems and prefer being slightly root-bound. Use a pot with excellent drainage holes. Fertilizer: Feed sparingly with a balanced liquid fertilizer diluted to half-strength once a month during the spring and summer. Do not fertilize in winter. Growth and Appearance Growth Habit: A compact, mounding plant that rarely exceeds 8 inches in height and width. Foliage: Features deeply corrugated, "quilted" leaves that are dark green above and maroon-red below. The leaves are arranged in a dense radial pattern. Flowers: Produces unique, upright, greenish-white flower spikes (spadices) that resemble mouse tails. Many growers snip these off to focus the plant's energy on the foliage. Propagation Leaf Cuttings: You can propagate by cutting a healthy leaf with a bit of stem and placing it in moist soil or water. Stem Cuttings: Take a cutting with at least one or two leaves and a node, and root it in a high-humidity environment. Division: Mature clumps can be gently divided into two or more plants during repotting. General Tips Air Purification: Like many houseplants, it helps remove toxins from the air and improves indoor air quality. Pet Safety: Non-toxic to cats and dogs according to the ASPCA, making it one of the safest colorful plants for pet owners. Maintenance: Occasionally wipe the leaves with a soft, dry cloth to remove dust. Avoid using leaf shine products, as they can clog the leaf pores. Terrarium Use: It is a favorite for midground placement in terrariums due to its slow growth and striking vertical color contrast.

name: Bucephalandra Helena 2014 (3 Rhizomes)
description: Bucephalandra "Helena 2014" Care Guide Bucephalandra "Helena 2014" Care Guide Bucephalandra "Helena 2014" is a legendary and highly coveted variety among aquatic plant collectors. Part of the "Helena" family, this specific 2014 variant is famed for its stunning transformation when submerged, evolving from a standard green into deep shades of dark purple, copper, and even reddish-brown. It is often considered a "sister" plant to the famous Brownie Ghost due to its intense coloration and distinct leaf texture. Here's a detailed guide to help you care for Bucephalandra "Helena 2014": Lighting Bucephalandra "Helena 2014" produces its best colors under medium to high-intensity lighting. While it can survive in low light, the plant will likely remain dark green. To achieve the signature deep purple and metallic hues, a high-quality full-spectrum LED light is necessary. Be mindful of the photoperiod (6-8 hours); as a slow-growing epiphyte, it is prone to Green Spot Algae (GSA) if the lighting is too intense without adequate CO2 and nutrient balance. Water Parameters Stability is the single most important factor for this rare variety to prevent rhizome melt. Ideal temperature: 71-79°F (21-26°C). {Link: According to www.2hraquarist.com https://www.2hraquarist.com/blogs/freshwater-aquarium-plants-guide/how-to-grow-bucephalandra}, keeping the water on the cooler side significantly reduces the risk of "Buce melt." pH range: 6.0-7.5. It thrives in soft to moderately hard water with a slightly acidic pH. Strong water circulation is highly beneficial, as it mimics its natural habitat in Borneo's fast-flowing streams and prevents organic debris from settling on the textured leaves. A GH of 5 or above and a KH of 1-4 will provide the necessary mineral foundation for healthy growth. CO2 and Fertilization CO2 injection is strongly recommended for "Helena 2014." It helps the plant grow more robustly and is essential for bringing out the vibrant, dark pigments. Regular liquid fertilization (dosing the water column) is required. Focus on micronutrients and iron to support the purple and red coloration. In low-tech setups without CO2, the plant will grow significantly slower and may lose its intense metallic sheen. Planting and Substrate Bucephalandra "Helena 2014" is an epiphyte. Do NOT bury the rhizome in the substrate, as it will rot and kill the plant. The best practice is to attach the plant to hardscape like lava rock, bogwood, or specialized ceramic mounts using super glue gel (cyanoacrylate) or cotton thread. Over time, its roots will anchor themselves firmly to the porous surfaces of your decor. Growth and Appearance This variety has a slow to medium growth rate. The leaves are typically oval to elliptical with slightly wavy edges. The appearance is characterized by a "velvety" texture and a deep, dark coloration that can range from midnight blue to royal purple under the right conditions. Like many premium Bucephalandra, the leaves are covered in tiny silvery dots (stomata) that shimmer like a "galaxy" when submerged. Propagation Propagation is done by dividing the rhizome. Use a sharp, sterilized blade to cut a section of the rhizome that has at least 3-5 healthy leaves and visible roots. Once divided, the new cutting can be attached to a new piece of hardscape where it will eventually begin to sprout new lateral buds. General Tips "Helena 2014" is a "collector's plant" and should ideally be placed in a mature, biologically stable aquarium. Expect some transition "melt" if the plant was grown emersed; however, do not discard the rhizome. As long as the rhizome is firm, it will sprout new submerged leaves adapted to your tank. Keep the aquarium clean with regular water changes to prevent the accumulation of waste, which can trigger algae outbreaks on the slow-growing foliage. Shrimp and snails are excellent tank mates as they will gently graze on the leaves, keeping them free of biofilm and dust.

name: Bucephalandra "Dark Catherine" (Rare, 3 Rhizomes)
description: Bucephalandra "Dark Catherine" Care Guide Bucephalandra "Dark Catherine" Care Guide Bucephalandra "Dark Catherine" (also commonly spelled Catherinae ) is a sophisticated and highly elegant variety from the island of Borneo. It is easily distinguished by its long, very narrow, and needle-like leaves that feature intense ruffling along the margins. As its name suggests, "Dark Catherine" displays a moody, deep forest-green to near-black coloration, often shimmering with a metallic purple or copper iridescence under high-quality lighting. This variety is a favorite at Aquatic Exotica for its refined texture and its ability to create a "willow-like" effect when flowing in the current of a professional aquascape. Lighting Intensity: Thrives in medium to high lighting . While it is more tolerant of lower light than some "Black" varieties, high intensity is required to bring out the dark purple metallic sheen. Spectrum: Full-spectrum RGB LEDs are recommended to highlight the "galaxy" spots (silvery stomata) and the deep, dark pigments of the narrow leaves. Photoperiod: Maintain a consistent 7–8 hour light cycle. Because it grows slowly, avoid excessive light without balanced CO2, as the thin leaves are susceptible to Green Spot Algae (GSA). Water Parameters Stability: Like all Buce, "Dark Catherine" hates sudden shifts in water chemistry. Regular, small water changes are better than infrequent large ones. Temperature: Prefers 71°F to 78°F (22°C – 26°C) . In warmer water, ensure high oxygenation to prevent rhizome rot. pH Range: 6.0 – 7.4. It thrives in slightly acidic to neutral water with low to medium hardness. Water Flow: Enjoys moderate to strong flow . The long, narrow leaves look beautiful waving in the current, and the flow helps keep the ruffled edges free of organic debris. CO2 and Fertilization CO2: Highly recommended. CO2 injection significantly improves the density of the ruffles and the speed of rhizome branching. Fertilization: It is a water-column feeder. Regular dosing of liquid macros and micros (NPK + Trace) is essential. Supplement with Iron (Fe) to deepen the dark coloration. Growth Rate: Very slow. It is a slow-motion grower that rewards patience with a dense, "spiky" architectural look. Planting and Substrate Epiphytic Nature: Do not bury the rhizome in the substrate. The rhizome must be exposed to the water column, or it will rot. Attachment: Secure it to Lava Rock, Driftwood, or Seiryu Stone using super glue gel (cyanoacrylate) or dark cotton thread. Its fine roots will eventually anchor it firmly to the hardscape. Placement: Due to its narrow, "grass-like" leaves, it is perfect for the foreground to midground or for accentuating the cracks between large rocks. Appearance and Texture Leaf Shape: Extremely narrow, linear, and elongated with a pointed tip. The edges are notably "crispy" or wavy. Coloration: Deep, dark olive-green to charcoal. Under optimal conditions, the new growth can appear reddish-brown before maturing into a dark, metallic purple. Form: Tends to grow in low, creeping mats that eventually form a thick, bushy clump of needle-like foliage. Propagation Division: Simply snip the rhizome with a sharp, clean blade. Cuttings: Ensure each piece has at least 4–6 leaves. Because the leaves are so narrow, a slightly longer rhizome section is often safer for propagation. Multi-Heading: Trimming the lead tip will often force the plant to sprout multiple new heads from the sides of the rhizome, creating a much fuller appearance. General Tips Cleanliness: Amano shrimp and Otocinclus are essential "clean-up crews" to keep the intricate, ruffled margins free from algae and biofilm. Melt Management: If the plant "melts" when first added to your tank, do not remove the rhizome. Clean off the dead leaves and keep the water stable; new, adapted growth will usually appear within 2–4 weeks. Design Tip: For Aquatic Exotica displays, pair "Dark Catherine" with bright green plants like Micranthemum 'Monte Carlo' to make the dark, needle-like leaves stand out.

name: Bucephalandra Galileo (Rare, 3 Rhizomes)
description: Bucephalandra "Galileo" Care Guide Bucephalandra "Galileo" Care Guide Bucephalandra "Galileo" is a captivating and relatively rare variety of Bucephalandra native to Borneo. It is highly prized for its unique leaf shape and the stunning iridescent colors it displays when submerged. Known for its scientific-sounding name, this variety is a star in high-tech aquascapes, offering a celestial shimmer of blues and purples on its dark foliage. Here's a detailed guide to help you care for Bucephalandra "Galileo": Lighting Bucephalandra "Galileo" performs best under medium lighting conditions. While it can tolerate lower light, higher intensity (within the medium range) is required to trigger the metallic iridescence that defines this variety. Full-spectrum LED lighting is recommended to highlight the blue and violet highlights on the leaf surfaces. Limit the photoperiod to 6-8 hours to prevent the buildup of algae on its slow-growing leaves. Water Parameters Like most premium Buce varieties, "Galileo" requires very stable water conditions to prevent rhizome melt. Ideal temperature: 72-78°F (22-26°C). {Link: According to www.buceplant.com https://buceplant.com/products/bucephalandra-galileo}, maintaining a stable, slightly cooler tropical temperature helps ensure the plant's longevity. pH range: 6.0-7.5. It prefers slightly acidic to neutral water. Good water flow is essential to mimic its natural environment in Borneo's streams and to keep the leaves free of organic waste. A dGH of 4-10 and a dKH of 2-5 provide a suitable mineral balance for healthy leaf development. CO2 and Fertilization CO2 injection is highly recommended for "Galileo" to achieve its maximum color potential and a faster growth rate. Without CO2, the plant may remain dark green and grow at an extremely slow pace. Regular dosing of a liquid fertilizer containing iron and micronutrients is vital for the health of this water-column feeder. Consistent nutrient availability prevents the leaves from developing holes or losing their vibrant sheen. Planting and Substrate Bucephalandra "Galileo" is an epiphytic plant and must not be planted with its rhizome buried in the substrate. Burying the rhizome will lead to rot and eventually the death of the entire plant. Attach the plant to hardscape materials such as lava rock, driftwood, or stones using super glue gel or thread. Over time, the roots will anchor themselves firmly to the surfaces of your aquarium décor. Growth and Appearance This variety is a slow grower, typical of the Bucephalandra genus. The leaves of "Galileo" are generally oval to elliptical with slightly wavy margins. Under proper lighting and CO2, the leaves develop a dark, velvety appearance with a distinct metallic blue or purple iridescence. Tiny silvery spots (stomata) are often visible on the leaf surface, appearing like a miniature galaxy when viewed closely. Propagation Propagation is achieved by dividing the rhizome using sharp, clean scissors or a blade. Ensure each divided section has a healthy piece of rhizome, at least 3-4 leaves, and some existing roots. Attach the new sections to hardscape to expand your colony or start new growth points in the tank. General Tips "Galileo" is sensitive to sudden changes in water chemistry; avoid introducing it to a newly set up, uncycled aquarium. If the plant begins to melt upon introduction, do not panic; remove the melting leaves but keep the rhizome, as it often sprouts new, adapted growth. Including algae-eating inhabitants like Amano shrimp or Nerite snails will help keep the leaves clean and free of biofilm. It is an excellent choice for the midground of an aquarium, especially when tucked into rock crevices where its colors can provide a focal point. It can be grown emersed in high-humidity setups, though its metallic colors are best viewed when submerged.

name: String of Frogs
description: Ficus pumila "Quercifolia" (String of Frogs) Care Guide String of Frogs (Ficus pumila "Quercifolia") Care Guide String of Frogs , scientifically known as Ficus pumila "Quercifolia" (or Miniature Oakleaf Fig), is a whimsical creeping vine beloved for its tiny, lobed leaves that resemble the silhouette of a frog or a miniature oak leaf. Unlike the larger-leafed Bucephalandra species, this is a terrestrial or semi-aquatic vining plant, making it a perfect addition to the terrestrial portions of a paludarium, terrarium, or as a trailing houseplant. Note: Be careful not to confuse this with the succulent Senecio radicans , which is also sometimes called "String of Frogs" but has very different care requirements (low humidity, infrequent watering). Lighting String of Frogs thrives in bright, indirect light. It can tolerate lower light levels, but its growth will slow significantly and the internodal spacing (the gap between leaves) will increase, making it look "leggy." Avoid direct afternoon sun, which can easily scorch the delicate, thin leaves. Morning sun or filtered light through a sheer curtain is ideal. Watering and Humidity Humidity: This plant loves high humidity (60-80%+). It is most successful in enclosed glass containers, vivariums, or near a humidifier. If the air is too dry, the leaf tips will quickly turn brown and crispy. Watering: Keep the substrate consistently moist but not waterlogged. Unlike succulents, it does not like to dry out completely. Water when the top 0.5 to 1 inch of soil feels slightly dry to the touch. In a terrarium setting, regular misting helps maintain the moisture levels the leaves crave. Temperature Ideal temperature range: 65-80°F (18-27°C). It is a tropical plant and is not frost-tolerant. Exposure to temperatures below 55°F (13°C) can cause leaf drop and stunted growth. Keep it away from cold drafts, air conditioning vents, or heaters that can dry out the surrounding air. Soil and Fertilization Substrate: Use a well-draining, moisture-retaining mix. A blend of potting soil, peat moss or coco coir, and perlite works well. In bioactive setups, it grows beautifully on sphagnum moss or directly on hardscape features like hygro-lon or cork bark if kept moist. Fertilizer: Feed once a month during the growing season (spring/summer) with a balanced liquid fertilizer diluted to half strength. Growth and Appearance Growth Habit: It is a vigorous, mat-forming creeper. It uses tiny aerial roots to cling to surfaces like wood, rock, or glass. Leaf Detail: The leaves are typically less than 0.5 inches long and have a distinct three-lobed shape that looks like a frog's foot or a tiny oak leaf. Versatility: It can be used as a ground cover, a trailing plant in a hanging basket, or a climbing plant on a background wall. Propagation Propagation is incredibly easy via stem cuttings. Simply snip a 2-3 inch section of the vine and place it on moist sphagnum moss or damp soil. Ensure the nodes (where the leaves meet the stem) are in contact with the moist surface; new roots will sprout from these points within 1-2 weeks in high-humidity conditions. General Tips Pruning: This plant can grow aggressively in a humid environment. Don't be afraid to trim it back to keep it from smothering slower-growing plants. Paludarium Use: While it loves moisture, the roots should not be submerged in water. Plant it in the "land" section and let it trail down toward the water line. Toxicity: Like most Ficus species, the sap can be a mild irritant to skin and is toxic to pets if ingested. Wear gloves if you have sensitive skin and keep it out of reach of cats and dogs.
